core_concept: World War II: Global Conflict and Its Aftermath
facts: World War II (1939-1945) was a global conflict involving every major power, not just European nations, because the Pacific War between Japan and the Allies expanded the war across Asia and the Pacific.
The Holocaust was the systematic murder of 6 million Jews by Nazi Germany, not a single event, because it was carried out through a network of camps, ghettos, and killing squads over several years.

The Pacific War ended after the United States dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945, not after a conventional invasion, because Japan refused to surrender until the bombs forced a capitulation.
The United Nations was created after WWII to replace the failed League of Nations, not as a wartime alliance, because the Allies wanted a permanent organization to prevent future global conflicts.

Germany was divided after WWII into East and West, not unified, because the victorious Allies (US, UK, France, and USSR) could not agree on a single government and split the country into occupation zones.
Decolonization accelerated after WWII because European powers were weakened by the war and could no longer maintain control over their colonies, not because they voluntarily granted independence.

The Cold War began immediately after WWII as a rivalry between the US and USSR, not as a continuation of WWII alliances, because the two superpowers disagreed over the future of Europe and global influence.
misconceptions: Students think the Holocaust was only about Jews being killed in concentration camps, but it also targeted millions of others including Roma, disabled people, and political prisoners.

Students believe the atomic bombs were dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki to end the Pacific War immediately, but Japan had already been considering surrender and the bombs were also meant to intimidate the Soviet Union.
Students think the United Nations was created during WWII as a military alliance, but it was founded in 1945 after the war ended to promote peace and cooperation among nations.
